metadata.subject: Social Studies
metadata.description: Social Justice 12 challenges students to explore the interconnected nature of social justice issues, from racism and poverty to LGBTQ+ rights, environmental justice, and gender equity. Students investigate the complexity of social injustice, examine how individual worldviews shape responses to these issues, and explore the tools — including activism, advocacy, law, and art — that individuals and organizations use to drive change.
metadata.general_description: An exploration of social justice issues including race, poverty, gender, LGBTQ+ rights, and environmental justice. Students analyze the causes of injustice, examine their own worldviews, and explore how individuals and systems can transform toward greater equity.

big_ideas.elaboration: Race, class, gender, disability, and sexuality are not separate issues — they intersect and compound each other, creating overlapping systems of privilege and oppression (intersectionality).

big_ideas.statement: Individual worldviews shape and inform our understanding of social justice issues.
big_ideas.elaboration: Our personal background, privilege, and life experiences shape how we see and respond to injustice — and recognizing this is a first step toward understanding.

big_ideas.statement: The causes of social injustice are complex and have lasting impacts on society.
big_ideas.elaboration: Systemic injustice is rooted in historical, economic, cultural, and political forces that reinforce each other — and whose effects are felt across generations.

big_ideas.statement: Social justice initiatives can transform individuals and systems.
big_ideas.elaboration: Activism, legal change, community organizing, and cultural shifts have all proven capable of dismantling unjust systems and creating more equitable ones.

categories.name: Use Social Studies inquiry processes and skills
categories.raw: Use Social Studies inquiry processes and skills to ask questions; gather, interpret, and analyze ideas; and communicate findings.

competencies.raw: Use Social Studies inquiry processes to investigate social justice issues and communicate findings.
i_can_statements: I can develop meaningful inquiry questions about a social justice issue.
I can gather information from diverse sources including lived experience, academic research, and advocacy organizations.
I can analyze data about inequality and present findings in a compelling format.
I can engage constructively in difficult conversations about injustice.

categories.name: Significance
categories.raw: Assess and compare the significance of people, places, events, or developments regarding social justice at particular times and places.

competencies.raw: Assess and compare the significance of people, events, or developments regarding social justice.
i_can_statements: I can explain why a particular activist, movement, or event was a turning point in a social justice struggle.
I can compare how social movements have achieved different levels of significance in different contexts.
I can argue for the enduring significance of a social justice achievement.

categories.name: Evidence
categories.raw: Assess the justification for competing accounts after investigating points of contention, reliability of sources, and evidence adequacy.

competencies.raw: Assess competing accounts of social justice issues by evaluating source reliability and evidence.
i_can_statements: I can evaluate a claim about inequality using statistical evidence.
I can identify when a source on a social justice topic is biased, incomplete, or misleading.
I can distinguish between lived experience, anecdotal evidence, and systemic data.

categories.name: Continuity and Change
categories.raw: Compare and contrast continuities and changes for different groups and individuals across times and places.

competencies.raw: Compare and contrast continuities and changes for different groups across times and places.
i_can_statements: I can trace how a particular form of discrimination has changed (or not changed) over time.
I can compare the experiences of a marginalized group in different countries or historical periods.
I can evaluate whether progress on social justice issues has been real and lasting.

categories.name: Cause and Consequence
categories.raw: Determine and assess long- and short-term causes and consequences of events, decisions, developments, policies, or movements.

competencies.raw: Determine and assess causes and consequences of social injustice, decisions, and movements.
i_can_statements: I can identify the root causes of a social justice issue — historical, economic, cultural, and structural.
I can trace the consequences of a policy or decision on a marginalized community.
I can evaluate whether a social justice initiative achieved its intended goals.

categories.name: Perspective
categories.raw: Explain different perspectives on past and present people, places, issues, and events.

competencies.raw: Explain different perspectives on past and present social justice issues.
i_can_statements: I can explain how a social justice issue looks different depending on your position of privilege or marginalization.
I can use the concept of intersectionality to analyze how multiple identities affect a person's experience.
I can present multiple perspectives on a controversial social justice topic fairly.

categories.name: Ethical Judgment
categories.raw: Make reasoned ethical judgments about controversial actions after considering context and standards of right and wrong.

competencies.raw: Make reasoned ethical judgments about controversial social justice actions and issues.
i_can_statements: I can make an ethical argument about a controversial social justice issue using more than one ethical framework.
I can consider competing values (e.g., free speech vs. protection from hate speech) in my ethical reasoning.
I can propose an ethical course of action in response to a social injustice.

areas.name: Definitions and Frameworks
areas.raw: Definitions, frameworks, and interpretations of social justice.
topics: Defining social justice: equity, equality, rights, and recognition
Frameworks: intersectionality (Kimberlé Crenshaw), structural racism, systemic oppression
The difference between equity and equality
Power, privilege, and positionality
Restorative vs. retributive justice

areas.name: Self-Identity and Relationships to Others
areas.raw: Self-identity and individual relationships to others.
topics: Social identity: race, gender, class, sexuality, ability, religion
Privilege and allyship
Implicit bias and unconscious prejudice
Empathy, active listening, and cross-cultural communication
Personal responsibility in addressing injustice

areas.name: Social Justice Issues
areas.raw: Social justice issues including race, poverty, LGBTQ+ rights, women's status, environmental justice, peace, globalization, disabilities.
topics: Racism and anti-racism: systemic and individual racism in Canada
Poverty and economic inequality: causes, effects, and responses
LGBTQ+ rights: history, current challenges, and legal protections
Gender equity: wage gap, reproductive rights, gender-based violence
Environmental justice: who bears the cost of environmental harm?
Disability rights: accessibility, inclusion, and representation
Peace and conflict: the social justice dimensions of war and militarism
Globalization and its human costs: labour rights, supply chains, exploitation

areas.name: Social Injustices in Canada and Globally
areas.raw: Social injustices in Canada and globally.
topics: Indigenous rights and reconciliation in Canada
Systemic racism in policing and the justice system
The over-representation of racialized people in poverty and incarceration
Historical and contemporary injustices: residential schools, apartheid, slavery
Global human rights violations: political prisoners, refugees, persecution

areas.name: Organizations Addressing Justice
areas.raw: Governmental and non-governmental organizations addressing justice.
topics: Government bodies: human rights commissions, ombudspersons, courts
NGOs: Amnesty International, Human Rights Watch, ACLU
Community organizations and grassroots groups
Social enterprises and ethical business
International organizations: UN, ILO, WHO

areas.name: Methods for Promoting Social Justice
areas.raw: Processes and methods individuals use to promote social justice.
topics: Protest and direct action: marches, strikes, civil disobedience
Political engagement: voting, lobbying, running for office
Art and culture as tools for change: music, film, literature
Digital activism and social media movements
Legal advocacy: constitutional challenges and landmark cases
Community organizing and coalition building
